name: bruteforce-luks
title: bruteforce-luks
summary: Try to find a password of a LUKS encrypted volume
description:
Try to find a password of a LUKS encrypted volume
The program is used to try discovery a password for encrypted LUKS volume used
to security reasons. It works trying decrypt at least one of the key slots by
trying all the possible passwords. It is used in forensics and is especially
useful if you know something about the password (i.e. you forgot a part of your
password but still remember most of it).
.
Because of cryptography complexity, crack the password of a LUKS volume without
knowing anything about it would take way too much time (unless the password is
really short and/or weak).

use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use bruteforce-luks because Try to find a password of a LUKS encrypted volume.
When the engagement is in the install phase, use bruteforce-luks as a cataloged Kali Linux tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `bruteforce-luks [options] <path to LUKS volume>` as documented upstream for bruteforce-luks.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `bruteforce-luks -t 4 -l 5 -m 5 /dev/sdb1` as documented upstream for bruteforce-luks.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `bruteforce-luks -t 8 -l 5 -m 10 -b "W4l" -e "z" /dev/sda2` as documented upstream for bruteforce-luks.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `bruteforce-luks -t 8 -l 10 -m 10 -s "P8" /dev/sdc3` as documented upstream for bruteforce-luks.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair bruteforce-luks with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `bruteforce-luks --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.

documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: sudo apt install bruteforce-luks. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `bruteforce-luks [options] <path to LUKS volume>`. Further invocations from upstream: `bruteforce-luks -t 4 -l 5 -m 5 /dev/sdb1`; `bruteforce-luks -t 8 -l 5 -m 10 -b "W4l" -e "z" /dev/sda2`. Try to find a password of a LUKS encrypted volume.

documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: bruteforce-luks - try to find the password of a LUKS volume.
